framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Establish and implement a risk management process to identify and mitigate risks throughout the AI lifecycle.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Protect AI systems and implement data governance measures to manage data quality and provenance.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Test AI models and systems to evaluate model performance and monitor the system once deployed.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Enable human control or intervention in an AI system to achieve meaningful human oversight.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Inform end-users regarding AI-enabled decisions interactions with AI and AI-generated content.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Establish processes for people impacted by AI systems to challenge use or outcomes.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Be transparent with other organisations across the AI supply chain about data models and systems to help them effectively address risks.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Keep and maintain records to allow third parties to assess compliance with guardrails.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Engage your stakeholders and evaluate their needs and circumstances with a focus on safety diversity inclusion and fairness.
notes: Precursor to mandatory guardrails; voluntary 2024 → expected mandatory bill 2026+

framework_name: Australian AI Safety Institute
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Where an AI system poses catastrophic irreversible or systemic risks alignment with AISI evaluation methodologies (eval suites red-team protocols) is recommended pre-deployment.
notes: New body; Microsoft founding industry partner announced 2026-04-24

framework_name: Australian AI Safety Institute
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_name: AI incident reporting to AISI register
control_description: Material AI incidents (harm at scale catastrophic failure model misuse) should be reported to the AISI incident register to inform sector-wide safety knowledge.
notes: New body; Microsoft founding industry partner announced 2026-04-24

framework_name: Australian AI Safety Institute
framework_publisher: Department of Industry Science and Resources
control_description: Deployment of frontier AI models in safety-critical or high-impact public-sector contexts is expected to be notified to AISI for pre-deployment review or post-deployment audit.
notes: New body; Microsoft founding industry partner announced 2026-04-24

framework_name: Human Rights Impact Assessment Guide
control_description: An HRIA is required where an AI system materially affects rights — vulnerable groups automated decisions on services surveillance use cases or irreversible-consequence systems.
notes: Cross-industry; embedded in NSW AIAF triggers and DTA AIAF impact assessment

framework_name: Human Rights Impact Assessment Guide
control_name: Map AI use to specific rights
control_description: HRIA must enumerate which human rights are engaged by the AI system (privacy non-discrimination freedom of expression fair trial etc.) with evidence of how each is impacted.
notes: Cross-industry; embedded in NSW AIAF triggers and DTA AIAF impact assessment

framework_name: Human Rights Impact Assessment Guide
control_description: For each engaged right mitigations must be documented and residual risks formally accepted by an accountable decision-maker. Residual risk acceptance must be revisited if the AI system materially changes.
notes: Cross-industry; embedded in NSW AIAF triggers and DTA AIAF impact assessment

framework_name: Human Rights Impact Assessment Guide
control_description: HRIA must include consultation with affected communities — particularly First Nations people with disability culturally and linguistically diverse communities and other groups identified as at-risk.
notes: Cross-industry; embedded in NSW AIAF triggers and DTA AIAF impact assessment

framework_name: Digital Platforms — AI transparency obligations
control_description: Digital services that use AI to generate content rank recommend or moderate must disclose AI involvement in plain language at the point of consumer interaction.
notes: Crosses gov + commercial; ACCC + DIGI guidance

framework_name: Digital Platforms — AI transparency obligations
control_description: Claims about AI capability (accuracy autonomy safety) must not be misleading or deceptive. Generic AI-powered marketing without substantiation may breach Australian Consumer Law.
notes: Crosses gov + commercial; ACCC + DIGI guidance

framework_name: Digital Platforms — AI transparency obligations
control_description: Synthetic media (deepfakes AI-generated text presented as human-authored) on digital platforms should carry provenance metadata or visible watermarks where used in advertising news or political content contexts.
notes: Crosses gov + commercial; ACCC + DIGI guidance

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that GenAI could lower barriers to creating or accessing chemical biological radiological or nuclear threat information. Requires red-team testing and content filtering for restricted-knowledge prompts.
mandatory_records: CBRN red-team results|content filter coverage|incident reports
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that GenAI generates erroneous content presented as factual. Requires factuality testing grounding mechanisms (retrieval source citation) and user-facing uncertainty signals.
mandatory_records: factuality eval results|grounding architecture docs|UX uncertainty signals
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk of generating content that incites violence promotes self-harm or expresses hatred. Requires output classification policy enforcement at generation time and continuous policy refresh.
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that training data exposure inference attacks or model memorisation leak personal data. Requires PII minimisation in training data extraction-attack testing and rate-limited inference.
mandatory_records: PII scrub records|membership-inference test results|inference rate limits
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk of disproportionate energy water and emissions from training and inference at scale. Requires measurement carbon-aware scheduling and model-size-vs-task fit reviews.
mandatory_records: energy and emissions metrics|model size justification|carbon-aware schedule
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that GenAI propagates stereotypes underrepresents groups or homogenises outputs across users. Requires bias testing across cohorts diversity metrics and counterfactual prompt evaluation.
mandatory_records: bias eval reports|cohort metrics|counterfactual test results
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk from over-reliance on AI outputs anthropomorphism inappropriate trust calibration or unclear handoff between human and AI. Requires UX disclosure handoff design and user training.
mandatory_records: UX disclosure content|handoff protocol|user training records
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk of GenAI being used to create disinformation deepfakes or manipulate information ecosystems. Requires provenance metadata watermarking detection systems and platform-level mitigation.
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that GenAI introduces new attack surfaces (prompt injection model extraction supply chain) or amplifies existing ones. Requires threat modelling adversarial testing and supply-chain assurance.
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk that training data or outputs infringe copyright trademark or other IP rights. Requires training-data lineage opt-out honouring output similarity testing and indemnification structures.
mandatory_records: training-data lineage|opt-out compliance log|similarity test results
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk of generating sexually explicit non-consensual or otherwise abusive content including CSAM-adjacent material. Requires hard-fail classifiers content blocklists and incident escalation paths.
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1)
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Risk inherited from foundation model fine-tuning data plug-in or agent-tool components. Requires component inventory upstream provenance and downstream-impact testing.
notes: Cross-industry; sits alongside NIST_AI_RMF_10 (parent framework) and applies to all GenAI deployments

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: The Board must ensure the institution has a Risk Management Framework that is appropriate for the size, business mix and complexity of operations. AI/ML model risk and AI-driven decisions form part of the institution-wide RMF, not a parallel system.
mandatory_records: Board-approved RMF document|RMF review schedule|board minutes
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: The Board must approve a Risk Appetite Statement covering all material risks including AI-driven model risk, third-party AI supplier risk, and AI fairness/bias risk. AI deployments must operate within the stated appetite.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: The Board is ultimately accountable for risk including AI-related risks. Cannot be delegated to executives or vendors. AI committees report to the Board Risk Committee.
mandatory_records: Board Risk Committee charter|AI committee terms of reference|reporting lines
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: A risk management function independent of business lines must oversee AI model risk, with the Chief Risk Officer accountable for AI risk reporting to the Board.
mandatory_records: CRO charter|AI model risk dashboard|independence attestation
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: The Board must promote a risk culture that includes responsible AI use. Speak-up channels for AI harm concerns must be operational; pressure to ship AI faster cannot override risk processes.
mandatory_records: Culture survey results|whistleblower reports|culture review minutes
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: AI model risk, AI third-party risk, AI bias risk, and AI operational risk must be classified as material where they could materially affect the institution. Classification reviewed annually.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: The annual Risk Management Declaration to APRA must address AI-related risks where material — coverage, controls, residual risk, treatment plan.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: Stress testing programs must include scenarios where AI systems fail, are biased at scale, or third-party AI providers degrade. Capital and liquidity impacts modeled where material.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: AI failure modes (model drift, hallucination, prompt injection, supplier outage) are operational risks; must be in the operational risk register with controls and KRIs.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: Any new product, service, or process using AI in a material way must go through new-product approval, including risk assessment, validation, and ongoing monitoring plan.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_description: Comprehensive independent review of the Risk Management Framework — including AI risk treatment — at least every three years; findings reported to the Board.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 220 — Risk Management
control_name: Notify APRA of material AI risk events
control_description: APRA must be notified of any material breach of the Risk Management Framework involving AI, including significant AI failures, mis-decisions affecting customers, or AI-driven control breakdowns.
notes: CPS 220 is non-AI-specific but applies to AI as a material risk

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_name: Clear information security roles for AI assets
control_description: The Board, senior management, governing bodies, and individuals must have clear information security roles and responsibilities. AI systems are information assets; their security ownership must be assigned by name.
mandatory_records: roles register|RACI for AI assets|Board attestation
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_name: Information security capability commensurate with AI exposure
control_description: Maintain information security capability commensurate with vulnerabilities and threats. AI introduces new attack surfaces (prompt injection, model extraction, training-data poisoning) that capability must cover.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_name: Information security policy framework includes AI
control_description: Information security policy framework must address AI-specific risks: AI model integrity, training-data sensitivity, AI output validation, AI supplier security.
mandatory_records: IS policy framework|AI security addendum|review schedule
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_name: AI assets in the information asset register
control_description: Information assets must be identified including AI models, training data, fine-tuning data, prompts, prompt logs, RAG corpora, embeddings stores, and AI inference endpoints.
mandatory_records: information asset register|AI asset classification|criticality rating
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Implement information security controls relative to vulnerabilities and threats — AI-specific controls include adversarial test gates, output validation, model artifact integrity, fine-tuning data lineage.
mandatory_records: control matrix|AI control evidence|control test results
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Information security controls must be effectively implemented; evidence (logs, metrics, attestations) maintained. Periodic re-validation required for AI controls because models and data drift.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Incident management capability must cover AI incidents — model exfiltration, prompt-injection at scale, data leakage via inference, biased decision incidents — including triage, containment, and lessons learned.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Systematic testing program covering all material AI security controls; includes red-team / adversarial testing of AI systems with severity-rated findings.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Internal audit independently reviews the design and operating effectiveness of AI security controls; findings reported to the Board Audit Committee.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_description: Where information assets are managed by third parties (AI vendors, foundation model providers, MLOps platforms), assess the design and operating effectiveness of their controls; contracts must support CPS 234 obligations.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Standard CPS 234 — Information Security
control_name: Notify APRA within 24 hours of material AI security incident
control_description: Material AI security incidents must be notified to APRA as soon as possible and not later than 72 hours; incidents that affect customers or services within 24 hours.
notes: CPS 234 explicitly applies to AI systems as information assets — most cited finance gov framework

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_name: Data governance for AI training and inference
control_description: Establish data governance arrangements including data ownership, data quality KPIs, and data lifecycle management for AI training data, fine-tuning data, prompts, and outputs.
mandatory_records: data governance framework|data owner register|quality KPIs
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_description: AI inputs must meet documented data quality criteria — accuracy, completeness, currency, consistency. Quality issues that materially affect AI outputs require remediation before continued use.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_description: Manage data through full lifecycle — capture, storage, use, archival, disposal — for AI training data, model artifacts, prompts, and outputs.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_name: AI data risks in the data risk register
control_description: AI-specific data risks (training-data leakage, inference-time PII exposure, fine-tuning data drift) must be in the data risk register with controls and treatment plans.
mandatory_records: data risk register|AI data risk entries|treatment plans
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_description: Maintain metadata standards covering data lineage, transformations, sensitivity classification, access controls — applied to AI training data and model artifacts.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_description: Least-privilege access controls on training data, fine-tuning corpora, prompts, and outputs; access reviewed periodically; segregation of duties for AI deployment.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_name: Data risk in AI outsourcing arrangements
control_description: Where data is held or processed by AI vendors (foundation model APIs, MLOps platforms), apply data risk principles to those arrangements; contracts must reflect data ownership and return obligations.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_name: Data architecture for AI scaled use
control_description: Data architecture must support AI use cases (feature stores, vector stores, model registries) without proliferating uncontrolled copies; central catalogue is mandatory above a defined threshold.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 235 — Managing Data Risk
control_description: Periodic independent assurance over data risk management for AI data — internal audit, external review, or specialist data risk function.
notes: CPG 235 is APRA guidance (not a standard) but treated as authoritative; applies to AI data

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Context of organisation includes AI use
control_description: Determine internal/external issues and interested parties relevant to the ISMS including AI use cases, AI suppliers, and AI-affected stakeholders.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_description: Top management must demonstrate leadership and commitment to ISMS including AI; ISMS policy must address AI risks.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_description: Plan to address risks and opportunities; AI-specific risks (model integrity, training-data sensitivity, supplier exposure) must be assessed and treated.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Resources, competence, awareness for AI security
control_description: Provide resources and competent personnel for AI security; awareness training covers AI-specific threats.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Operational planning + control of AI changes
control_description: Operational planning and control includes AI deployment, changes, and supplier relationships managed under change-control.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Monitoring and measurement of AI controls
control_description: Monitor, measure, analyse, evaluate ISMS performance; AI-specific KPIs (control coverage, drift, incidents) tracked.
mandatory_records: KPI dashboards|internal audit reports|management review records
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_description: Continual improvement applies to AI security controls; non-conformities trigger corrective action.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Annex A.5 organisational controls for AI
control_description: 37 organisational controls — policies, roles, intelligence, asset management, access control — applied to AI assets where relevant.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Annex A.6 people controls for AI users
control_description: 8 people controls — screening, terms of employment, awareness, disciplinary process — extended to AI users and admins.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Annex A.7 physical controls for AI infrastructure
control_description: 14 physical controls — premises, equipment, secure disposal — applied to AI infrastructure including GPU servers and data archives.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Annex A.8 technological controls for AI systems
control_description: 34 technological controls — secure development, vulnerability management, network controls, cryptography, logging — directly applicable to AI systems.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_description: Where the organisation also adopts ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System), integrate the two management systems to avoid duplicate assurance.
mandatory_records: integrated management system documentation|joint audit plan
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_description: Where the organisation seeks ISO 27001 certification, ensure AI systems are within scope and the certificate references AI inclusion explicitly.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — Information Security Management System
control_name: Information security in supplier relationships including AI vendors
control_description: Information security requirements (Annex A.5.19-A.5.23) extend to AI suppliers — foundation model providers, MLOps platforms, AI-embedded SaaS.
notes: ISO 27001:2022 is the global ISMS baseline; cross-cited by APRA, NIST, ASIC, ACSC

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: GOVERN — establish and monitor cybersecurity strategy including AI
control_description: Establish, communicate, and monitor cybersecurity risk strategy, expectations, and policy. NEW in 2.0 — explicitly elevates governance. AI risks form part of the strategy.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Cybersecurity risk management strategy must address AI risks — AI-driven attacks, AI as attack surface, AI in defence.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: IDENTIFY — asset management including AI assets
control_description: Develop the organisational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, assets, data, capabilities — including AI models, training data, AI services.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: Identify — supply chain risk for AI vendors
control_description: Identify supply chain risk; AI-specific concerns include foundation model provenance, training-data sources, sub-processor disclosure.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services; AI-specific safeguards: input filtering, output validation, model artifact integrity, RBAC on training data.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: Protect — data security for AI training and inference
control_description: Manage data consistent with risk strategy — AI training data classification, encryption at rest/transit, secure disposal of fine-tuning corpora.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: DETECT — anomaly detection for AI behaviour
control_description: Develop and implement appropriate activities to detect cybersecurity events. For AI: model drift detection, anomalous prompt patterns, privilege escalation via AI agents.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_name: Detect — adverse event monitoring for AI
control_description: Continuous monitoring for adverse AI events: hallucination at scale, output bias drift, suspicious agent tool-use patterns.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Develop and implement appropriate activities to act regarding a detected cybersecurity event. AI incident response addresses model rollback, data quarantine, stakeholder communication.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Mitigation contains incidents; for AI, mitigations may include taking the model offline, falling back to deterministic logic, notifying affected customers.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Develop and implement appropriate activities to maintain plans for resilience and to restore capabilities or services impaired by a cybersecurity incident, including AI services.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
framework_publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
control_description: Where applicable, align with NIST AI RMF and NIST CSF jointly via a CSF "Profile" that captures both cyber and AI risk treatment.
notes: CSF 2.0 (Feb 2024) elevates Govern as new top-level function; widely adopted in AU finance

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: AI systems must be inside the AML/CTF Program (Part A)
control_description: If AI is used in transaction monitoring, customer risk scoring, sanctions screening, or KYC, the AI systems must be part of the risk-based Part A AML/CTF Program. The Program must describe the AI system, its data inputs, decisioning role, human-oversight points, and tested effectiveness.
mandatory_records: AML/CTF Part A program document|AI inventory|AI risk assessment|effectiveness testing records
source_section: AML/CTF Act 2006 Pt 7 Division 2 + Rules Chapter 8
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Seven-year record-keeping for AI-driven AML decisions
control_description: AI-generated alerts, AI-driven KYC outcomes, AI risk-score history, and AI sanctions-screening match decisions must be retained for at least 7 years and be retrievable by case. Model versions and decision parameters at the time of each decision must be reconstructable.
mandatory_records: alert ledger 7y|model version registry|decision-time parameter snapshots|retrieval test evidence
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Suspicious Matter Report explainability for AI-flagged matters
control_description: Where AI is the primary signal that triggers a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR), the SMR must contain a human-readable explanation of why the matter is suspicious, including the role AI played and the human review that confirmed it. AI alone is insufficient — a human must form the suspicion.
source_section: AML/CTF Act 2006 s41 + AUSTRAC SMR Guidance
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Generative-AI explanations must respect tipping-off offence
control_description: Section 123 makes it an offence to disclose to a customer that an SMR has been or may be filed. Generative AI used in customer-facing channels (chatbots, email assistants, KYC explanations) must be gated against revealing — directly or indirectly — that a customer is under suspicion or being reported. Cited refusals are required.
mandatory_records: chatbot policy gates|tipping-off red-team test results|cited-refusal audit trail
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Independent review must cover AI in the AML program
control_description: Where AI is part of the AML/CTF Program Part A, the independent review (every 2-3 years for most reporting entities) must explicitly assess the AI: model risk, alert effectiveness, false-positive/negative profile, bias, override rates, and human-oversight quality. The reviewer must have AI competence.
mandatory_records: independent review terms of reference|AI scope inclusion evidence|reviewer credentials|review report
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Model validation for AML AI on a defined cycle
control_description: AI/ML models used for transaction monitoring, customer risk scoring, sanctions/PEP screening, or KYC must be validated on a defined cycle (typically annually for high-impact models) by a function independent of model development. Validation must cover data drift, performance drift, threshold appropriateness, and bias.
source_section: AUSTRAC Industry Guidance — Use of AI/ML in AML 2024
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Transaction-monitoring alert audit trail with AI provenance
control_description: Every alert generated by an AI transaction-monitoring model must be auditable end-to-end: which model, which version, which scenario/feature triggered, which data window, who reviewed, what disposition, and why. Sampling-based assurance is acceptable but must be documented.
mandatory_records: alert ledger with model+version|scenario library|review disposition log|sampling-assurance plan
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Sanctions and PEP screening AI thresholds documented
control_description: AI-based sanctions, PEP, and adverse-media screening must have documented match thresholds, fuzzy-match algorithms, false-positive/negative tolerances, and escalation paths. Thresholds must be calibrated against sanctioned-name list updates and reviewed at least annually.
mandatory_records: screening configuration|threshold rationale|annual recalibration evidence|sanction-list update log
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: AI-driven KYC: identity verification quality controls
control_description: Where AI performs document verification, biometric matching, or identity proofing as part of customer-due-diligence (CDD), the model accuracy, false-accept/false-reject rates, and bias profile (across demographics) must be measured, documented, and meet AUSTRAC's risk-based standard. Manual fallback paths must exist.
mandatory_records: KYC AI accuracy reports|FAR/FRR by cohort|bias audit|manual-review fallback procedure
source_section: AML/CTF Rules Chapter 4 + AUSTRAC Identity Verification Guidance
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Customer risk-score AI explainability and human override
control_description: Customer risk scores produced by AI must be explainable to a compliance reviewer in business language and must be overridable by a human compliance officer with documented reason. Scores must drive enhanced-due-diligence (EDD) workflows where high-risk; AI alone cannot determine EDD applicability without human concurrence.
mandatory_records: score explanation traces|override register|EDD trigger log
source_section: AML/CTF Rules Pt 4.4 — Customer Risk Rating
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Threshold transaction and IFTI reporting accuracy with AI
control_description: Where AI assists in identifying threshold transaction reports (TTR ≥ AUD 10K cash) or international funds transfer instructions (IFTI), the classification accuracy must be monitored. Missing TTRs/IFTIs are reportable breaches; the entity must have AI fallback monitoring to detect classification gaps.
mandatory_records: TTR submissions|IFTI submissions|AI classification accuracy log|missed-report breach register
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Generative AI in SMR drafting requires human-in-the-loop
control_description: Where generative AI assists in drafting SMR narratives, the human compliance officer remains accountable. AI drafts must be reviewed and edited before submission; the SMR must reflect the human's professional judgement, not the model's output verbatim. Audit trail must distinguish AI-drafted vs human-edited content.
mandatory_records: AI-drafting audit log|human-edit diff record|SMR submission attestation
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Customer data sovereignty for AI processing
control_description: Customer data processed by AI for AML/CTF purposes should remain within Australian jurisdiction or other adequate jurisdictions. Use of US-hosted commercial LLMs for processing identifiable customer transaction data triggers Privacy Act APP 8 cross-border disclosure obligations and may conflict with AUSTRAC's expectation of data control.
mandatory_records: data flow map|cross-border disclosure register|LLM hosting location attestation
source_section: Privacy Act APP 8 + AUSTRAC Cloud and Outsourcing Guidance
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: AUSTRAC AML/CTF Act and Industry Guidance — AI/ML in AML
framework_publisher: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
control_name: Tranche 2 reporting entities — AI in AML uplift
control_description: From 2026, Tranche 2 expansion brings lawyers, accountants, real-estate agents, and dealers in precious metals/stones into AUSTRAC's reporting-entity perimeter. Where AI is deployed (client-onboarding chatbots, document automation, KYC), the same Part A program, record-keeping, SMR, and tipping-off obligations apply. New reporting entities should design AI controls now.
mandatory_records: Tranche 2 readiness plan|AI inventory for new reporting entities|AML program for designated services
source_section: AML/CTF Amendment (Making Australia More Secure) Act 2024
notes: AUSTRAC AI/AML coverage; effective date is AML/CTF Act 2006 commencement; specific AI guidance issued 2024-2025; Tranche 2 expansion 2026

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Provide financial services efficiently, honestly and fairly when using AI
control_description: AFSL holders must do all things necessary to ensure financial services are provided efficiently, honestly and fairly. Where AI is used to deliver, recommend, price, or document financial services, the AI's behaviour, accuracy, and outcomes must support — not undermine — these obligations. Documentary evidence of AI fairness and accuracy testing is expected at audit.
mandatory_records: AI fairness testing|customer-outcome reviews|complaints register linked to AI decisions|board attestation
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Adequate resources to deploy and oversee AI
control_description: AFSL holders must have adequate financial, technological, and human resources to provide the financial services and to carry out supervisory arrangements. Adopting AI does not reduce this duty — it expands it. Resource adequacy includes AI-skilled staff, model-risk specialists, and continuous-monitoring capability.
mandatory_records: resource adequacy assessment|AI skills register|model risk function staffing|monitoring tooling inventory
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Risk management systems must cover AI risk
control_description: AFSL holders must have adequate risk management systems. AI introduces model risk, hallucination risk, prompt-injection risk, data-leakage risk, and concentration risk via shared LLM providers — all of which must be inside the risk management framework, with named owners and quantified appetite.
mandatory_records: risk register with AI categories|risk appetite statement|model risk policy|RCSA covering AI
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Representatives adequately trained in AI tools and limitations
control_description: AFSL holders must ensure representatives are adequately trained and competent. Where representatives use AI tools (drafting, advice support, customer comms, claims triage), training must cover the tool's accuracy profile, failure modes, prohibited uses (tipping-off, advice without disclosure), and escalation paths.
mandatory_records: AI training curriculum|attestation records|competency assessments|tool-use policy
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Breach reporting for AI-caused breaches under s912D
control_description: Reportable situations (significant breaches, gross negligence, serious fraud, ongoing breaches) must be reported to ASIC within 30 calendar days. AI-caused incidents — incorrect advice at scale, biased pricing, mis-classified claims, leaked PII — that meet the reportable-situation threshold must be reported. RG 78 applies.
mandatory_records: breach register with AI root-cause tagging|RG 78 reportable-situation assessment|ASIC submissions|root-cause analysis
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Internal dispute resolution must handle AI-decision complaints
control_description: AFSL holders must have an IDR procedure compliant with RG 271. Complaints about AI-driven decisions (e.g. AI-rejected applications, AI-priced premiums, AI-generated advice) must be capable of human review with full decision provenance, must respond within statutory timeframes, and must support escalation to AFCA.
mandatory_records: AI decision provenance for IDR|complaint outcomes by AI category|RG 271 timeframe compliance|AFCA referrals
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Design and Distribution Obligations — AI must respect TMD
control_description: DDO requires issuers and distributors to design financial products with a Target Market Determination (TMD) and distribute consistently with it. Where AI selects, prioritises, recommends, or markets products, the AI's outputs must be constrained to TMD-eligible customers; AI-driven personalisation must not push products outside the TMD.
mandatory_records: TMD-AI alignment review|distributor reporting|complaints linked to TMD breach|AI recommendation logs
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Digital and AI-driven personal advice — RG 255 conformance
control_description: Digital advice (including AI-driven robo-advice) is personal financial advice when it factors a client's circumstances. RG 255 expectations apply: organisational competence, monitoring of algorithmic outputs, version control of advice models, scenario testing, and ongoing review of advice quality. Best-interests duty (BID) applies to AI-assisted personal advice.
mandatory_records: advice model registry|BID-compliance evidence|advice scenario testing|advice quality review|RG 255 attestation
source_section: ASIC RG 255 — Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: Best Interests Duty applies when AI assists personal advice
control_description: Personal advice providers must act in the client's best interests (s961B). AI assistance in research, suitability, or recommendation does not transfer this duty. The adviser must be able to demonstrate the AI's input was reviewed, contextualised, and where used, was the right information for this client's needs.
mandatory_records: BID step records|adviser-AI review notes|client-record-of-advice with AI-source attribution
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: AI-generated marketing must not be misleading or deceptive
control_description: Misleading and deceptive conduct prohibitions (s12DA, s1041H) apply to AI-generated content. AI-drafted product disclosure statements, advertisements, social-media posts, and customer communications must be reviewed for accuracy and accuracy of implied claims (e.g., return projections, risk descriptions, suitability statements).
mandatory_records: AI-content review log|claim-substantiation files|marketing approval workflow|customer-impact monitoring
source_section: ASIC Act 2001 s12DA + Corporations Act s1041H
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: AI in insurance claims handling — fairness and prompt resolution
control_description: Where AFSL extends to insurance claims handling and settling services (since 2021 reforms), AI-driven claim triage, fraud detection, or settlement-amount calculation must support efficient, honest and fair claim outcomes. Adverse AI-driven outcomes must be reviewed by a human before customer notification.
mandatory_records: claim-AI decision logs|human review attestation|adverse-outcome notification log|claim-cycle-time metrics
source_section: Corporations Act 2001 s912A + Insurance Contracts Act + ASIC INFO 253
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: AI-driven suitability and onboarding must reflect actual customer circumstances
control_description: Where AI scores, classifies, or recommends products to retail clients during onboarding, the AI's inputs (income, dependants, goals, risk tolerance) must be evidenced. Where the AI uses inferred attributes, the inference and its basis must be transparent and auditable. Customers must have a path to correct erroneous AI-derived attributes.
mandatory_records: suitability-input audit|inferred-attribute log|customer-correction register|product matching evidence
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_description: Under RG 105, AFSL holders must show they have the knowledge and skills to provide their licensed services. Where AI is integral to service delivery, organisational competence must include AI/ML competence at the responsible-manager level — sufficient to oversee third-party AI vendors, model behaviour, and emerging AI risks.
mandatory_records: responsible manager AI competence evidence|skills matrix|external AI advisor engagements|RG 105 attestation
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274

framework_name: ASIC Australian Financial Services Licence (AFSL) — General Obligations and AI
control_name: AI must not introduce undisclosed conflicts of interest
control_description: AFSL holders must manage conflicts of interest. Where AI ranks, recommends, or steers customers towards products, the AI's training data, optimisation objectives, and any commercial weighting (e.g., favouring in-house products) must be transparent and disclosed. Hidden product-selection bias is a conflict.
mandatory_records: AI optimisation-objective documentation|training-data sources|disclosed steering policies|conflict register
notes: AFSL general obligations under Corporations Act s912A; effective date is FSR Act commencement; AI-specific guidance via ASIC INFO 225, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 255, RG 271, RG 274
